title: Adaptive Robustness of Hypergrid Johnson-Lindenstrauss
speaker: Andrej Bogdanov
abstract:
In 1984 W. B. Johnson and J. Lindenstrauss showed that a random projection of an arbitrary point set S into low-dimensional space is approximately distance-preserving, as long as S is of size at most exponential in the target dimension. The resulting embedding has found many uses in data science.
If S is larger than exponential, however, its points can contract arbitrarily under the projection.
We give evidence that when S is the n-dimensional hypergrid of integral points with bounded  infinity-norm, the task of finding a contracting pair of points exhibits a computational-statistical gap. In a certain parameter range, contracting pairs are abundant, but finding such a pair is computationally infeasible.
As a cryptographic application, we show that the rounded Johnson-Lindenstrauss embedding is a robust property-preserving hash function. Such hash functions h compress data while preserving distances between inputs up to some distortion factor, with the guarantee that even knowing h, no computationally bounded adversary can find a pair of points that violates the distortion bound.
The talk is based on joint work with Alon Rosen, Neekon Vafa, and Vinod Vaikutanathan.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian Johnson-Lindenstrauss motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the Gaussian-matrix version of the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma and motivates robustness questions through low-dimensional image hashing and sensitivity versus invariance attacks.

proof_stages.label: Hypergrid contraction formulation
proof_stages.summary: Because a real linear map has a kernel, the invariance attack becomes meaningful only after restricting inputs to a bounded integer grid, leading to the problem of finding a nonzero grid vector whose Gaussian image is unusually short.

proof_stages.label: Statistical and computational phase landscape
proof_stages.summary: The parameters alpha, grid bound b, and contraction kappa are organized into no-solution, existence-but-hard, and algorithmic regimes, with a rounded-output version giving a discrete shrinking map whose collisions exist but may be hard to find.

proof_stages.label: Kernel-sampling rounding algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The first natural algorithm samples a vector in the continuous kernel of the Gaussian matrix and snaps it to the nearest grid point, yielding contraction on the order of 1/b but not exploiting small alpha.

proof_stages.label: Online vector balancing and cooling algorithm
proof_stages.summary: Viewing the matrix columns as a stream, a greedy sign-balancing dynamics has limiting output norm about sqrt(pi/8)m for plus-minus-one entries, and a multiscale cooling variant on the b-grid achieves contraction about sqrt(alpha)/b.

proof_stages.label: Lattice and CLWE hardness in flat regimes
proof_stages.summary: For very flat matrices, an algorithm beating the sqrt(alpha)/b contraction scale would imply an algorithm for standard lattice short-basis-type problems via continuous-LWE-related reductions.

proof_stages.label: Overlap-gap umbrella obstruction
proof_stages.summary: In the constant-parameter regime, the speaker invokes overlap-gap heuristics and proves nonexistence of certain umbrella configurations, which rules out better norm-concentrated online algorithms by a correlated-instances branching argument.

proof_stages.label: Planted hidden-collision application
proof_stages.summary: The computational/statistical gap is used in follow-up work to sample a matrix statistically close to Gaussian together with a hidden short vector, enabling a planted near-collision or backdoor that ordinary algorithms should not find.

examples.name: Near-neighbor image search after dimension reduction
examples.note: The speaker uses image near-neighbor search to illustrate why a Johnson-Lindenstrauss embedding is algorithmically useful before any robustness issue is introduced.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites near-neighbor image search as a motivating application of embedding images into lower dimension, without carrying out a concrete search or calculation

examples.name: Apple NeuralHash attacks
examples.note: Apple's approximately linear image hash and reported near-collision attacks motivate the move from ordinary JL embeddings to contraction attacks on discretized linear maps.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes Apple NeuralHash and reported sensitivity/invariance attacks as motivation, but does not analyze that implementation concretely

passes.reason: The NeuralHash attacks are presented as adversarial near-collision/sensitivity attacks showing a deployed hashing approach can be broken.
Presented as adversarial near-collision/sensitivity attacks showing NeuralHash-style approximate linear hashing can fail robustness.
adversarial NeuralHash attacks exhibit robustness failures via sensitivity or near-collision behavior
ordinary adversarial attacks used to show NeuralHash-style embeddings can be broken by sensitivity or near-collision failures
The NeuralHash attacks are used as adversarial cases showing a robustness/security failure via near-collisions, but are not structurally exceptional mathematical objects.
The NeuralHash attacks are adversarial near-collision/sensitivity cases used to show a robustness failure, with no intrinsic exceptional structure.
Presented as adversarial sensitivity/invariance attacks producing near-collisions that show NeuralHash-style robustness can fail.
The NeuralHash attacks are adversarial near-collision/sensitivity cases showing the hash’s robustness can fail, but they are structurally ordinary attacks.
Used as adversarial near-collision/sensitivity attacks showing that NeuralHash-style robustness can fail, while not being structurally exceptional.
The NeuralHash attacks are adversarial near-collision examples showing a robustness failure of an approximately linear image hash.

examples.name: SHA-style collision-resistant hashes
examples.note: In response to a question, standard engineered cryptographic hash functions are contrasted with distance-preserving embeddings, illustrating why ordinary collision resistance is not the goal here.
examples.engagement_reason: SHA-style collision-resistant hashes are only invoked in response to a question as engineered hashes that would not preserve distances

passes.reason: SHA-style hashes are invoked as an engineered collision-resistant alternative that does not solve the geometric distance-preservation/isometry goal.
Used as a non-solution to the proposed construction: SHA-style collision resistance does not give the needed JL-like distance preservation.

Cited to rebut the idea that ordinary collision-resistant hashes like SHA solve the distance-preserving embedding problem, since they likely fail isometry.

examples.name: Fashion-MNIST backdoor demonstration
examples.note: A neural network with a planted Gaussian first-layer collision is illustrated on clothing/shoe images to show how a visually large perturbation can be made close in the learned representation.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes running the planted-collision/backdoor construction in a Fashion-MNIST-style neural-network demo and reasons through how modified images remain close in the embedding output

passes.reason: The Fashion-MNIST construction demonstrates a planted backdoor/near-collision that makes a classifier treat visually altered inputs as close.
